reviews.review_text:
Slay the Spire is what happens when a deck-building game looks at your free time, your sanity, and your fragile human hope, then politely asks if it can borrow them forever—and you say yes because the cards make a nice *shuffling sound*.

You begin as a humble adventurer climbing a cursed tower. Within minutes, you are no longer an adventurer—you are a gambling addict in medieval cosplay, desperately praying that your next card reward contains something that doesn’t actively ruin your life. It won’t.

The core gameplay loop is simple:

1. Fight a monster.
2. Get a card.
3. Realize you’ve made your deck worse.
4. Repeat until emotional collapse.

Every decision feels meaningful, which is a polite way of saying every mistake feels like it was handcrafted by a malicious deity who knows your exact breaking point. You’ll carefully construct a “perfect” deck, only to watch it get disassembled by a mushroom with anger issues or a floating orb that clearly has a degree in physics and hatred.

The relics—oh, the relics. These shiny little trinkets promise power, but often deliver consequences. “Gain energy each turn,” they whisper, “but also never rest again, idiot.” It’s like signing a contract written in blood and poor life choices. And you will take it. Every time.

Combat is turn-based, which gives you plenty of time to think about what you’ve done. You’ll hover over your cards, calculating the optimal play like a genius strategist, only to end your turn and realize you’ve miscounted energy and are about to get punched into another dimension by a sentient slab of concrete.

And then there’s the RNG. Sweet, beautiful, completely unhinged RNG. Sometimes it blesses you with godlike synergy and you ascend the Spire as a radiant being of destruction. Other times, it hands you a deck so dysfunctional it could qualify for government assistance.

But the real horror—the *true* darkness—comes from the fact that every failure is technically your fault. The game is balanced. The mechanics are fair. The only variable is you, and you are terrible.

In conclusion, Slay the Spire is a masterclass in game design, psychological warfare, and teaching players that hope is a resource best spent sparingly. It will consume your time, your patience, and your will to live—and you’ll thank it for the privilege.

10/10, I climbed the Spire and all I got was this crippling addiction and a deep mistrust of card games.
Great game. One minute its 6pm, next minute its 3am and you're still dying to Book of Stabbing.

Best [h3] dopamine [/h3] game ever

I completed the main run of Slay the Spire for the first time after about 14 hours of gameplay, and it finally made me understand why so many people speak so highly of it. At first, the game can seem simple, but little by little its depth becomes clear. Every run feels different, and the combination of cards, relics, and enemies constantly pushes you to adapt and think ahead. Few games manage to make failure feel this motivating, where even a lost run teaches you something for the next attempt.

What impressed me most is how balanced and rewarding the progression feels. Building a deck that slowly becomes stronger and more efficient is incredibly satisfying, especially when everything finally comes together near the end of a run. Each character offers a very different playstyle, giving the game a huge amount of variety and replay value. Finishing the game for the first time felt less like reaching the end and more like opening the door to everything else it has to offer. I will definitely keep playing and try to get the platinum, because this feels like the kind of game that only gets better the more you understand it — and I’ll be sure to update this review once I finally achieve it.

Its a fun, simple, rouguelike card game. What more could a girl want?
This game is so awesome, with great theming, ambience, creatures, and gameplay. There is simply so much replayability in this game, with every climb being different and uniquely challenging. 
10/10, exactly what I paid for, no notes

Fantastic game.

Artstyle of this game held me back from trying it until it went 90% off and I bought it. Still after winter sale I gave it a try this time and I'm amazed by it's gameplay and various cards which are all well made!

What I liked about this game they give debuff on enemies to progress faster if you are trying again and again which is not boring/burn out feel and it's really cool!

Can't wait to beat the game with other characters as well as after finishing this 1st I will get 2nd game <3
Must play Slay the Spire!
This game is amazing. Slay the Spire 2 is also amazing. I should have left a recommended review years ago.

this game, by any metric, is a seriously impressive feat of game balancing. i recommend it to anyone. literally, anyone. i mostly like blue, but the other colors are great too. but let me give you one heartfelt piece of advice. this applies to most, if not all games, but this one especially. do not look into or read up on guides, deck types to build, tips on how to win, etc. it will completely ruin what is the fun of the game, ie figuring stuff out for yourself. sure, this applies to any game. but for this game especially, you can turn what is a hundreds if not thousands of hoursworth game into a game you got bored of within a day. if you don't ruin yourself on the replayability of the game, though, expect to have a WHALE of a time (haha) figuring it out on you're own, experimenting with combos/builds and so on. at first, the game seems brutally difficult. how is anyone supposed to survive any of this? but soon enough, you start to figure it out and before you know it you're winning most of your runs. 10/10

i play this whenever i have nothing to play because im so confused which games to play but this game just click click click card oop im dead restart i win next level I DIED keep going ironclad and the other three I WON I WON I WON the heart kicked my head in

Losing in this game usually doesn't feel deserved. A game is usually good when I lose and see where I made mistakes or could have played better. This might as well be gambling. Multiple runs I've come up with crazy powerful decks that just wipe everything out just to get obliderated at some point. It just feel like a waste of time. People love it because it really does give an illusion of having a lot of control. Unless you're autistic you're runs will just feel like you were doing great at a boardgame and someone just flipped the table. The very first run I ever did I won. After that I lost about 10 in a row and a bunch of those I had a far better deck. Tell me how in HELL that makes sense without heavy RNG. I now have 17 losses and 3 wins. A majority of those losses simply put me in a situation where the numbers didn't add up. There was no play to win with the deck I had. Fights in tight spaces is a better game which gives you more control and doesn't feel like the losses were completely out of your control. This game is why gambling is an issue for people because it plays soo good that it gives a heavy illusion of control.

I am going to go against the grain and begin by saying that: No, I will never recommend this game to anyone ever.

Your goal is to build a deck of cards and ascend to the top. There are 3 floors, 4 floors for the final boss. The final boss requires a very specific setup in order to even have a shot at defeating, due to the boss mechanics. In planning around the final boss, you will almost always die before you even reach the boss. So you're stuck in a catch 22 where you have to build around the final boss, YET somehow be able to survive everything on the way up. 

Then you realize that there's no break before the boss, so there will be moments where the run is over even long before you get to the boss room. And don't even get me started on how the HP resource is in this game, where simple monster encounters can literally end your career just because you can have a good deck of cards but happened to draw all the worst ones.

I am well aware that this game is a rougelike, hence RNG is a core element of the game. Except that the RNG in this game is the worst I've ever seen when it comes to the cards you get, relics, floor layouts, and what not. I can already see myself getting memed/clowned on/"skill issue" comments, but thats water off a duck's back to me. I will still 100% this game eventually, but it is unlkely that my opinion on this game will ever change.

86%. For reference, I started this review about 15 hours in, fresh off my first Silent win, thinking it’d be a nice time capsule from my time as a Slay the Spire noob to when I inevitably reach several hundred hours of playtime. However, this game is so addicting that I doubled that time until I beat the game with each character.

Even after over 30 hours, I’m only scratching the surface of each character’s playstyle, which could be a deterrent for those who need to quickly understand game mechanics (or I’m just dumb). But the learning process is very fun.

At first I wasn’t sold on STS when I played an unsuccessful run. This is a highly esteemed roguelike, but doesn’t have the constant action of RoR, the hype power ups of Isaac, or the punchy visuals of Hades. Instead, it’s a card game where you need to pull out your old TI-80 calculator to win. 

But that relative simplicity in its presentation makes STS  a lot more casual to play than those three games I listed, which I find to be consistently stressful, especially since STS gives you great leeway to choose when to stop playing. I’d rather boot this game up than other roguelikes since I can play at my own pace and take my time comprehending all the randomness around me. 

I would strangely describe STS as a “MacGyver jigsaw puzzle” game, even if the analogy is held together by a thread. You scrounge up what you can find, start a fight, and must figure out how to survive the fight by any means with your current build. There isn’t a crazy amount of fights in each floor, which leads to each one feeling like distinct and memorable ways of challenging your builds into being flexible enough to account for them.

[i]How[/i] you survived a fight feels self-earned through whatever decisions led you to attaining whatever cards, relics, and potions that helped you, with the help from a little RNG fairy on your draws. If you’ve ever completed a big jigsaw puzzle, it’s that same hit of euphoria.

While 99.99+% of runs are winnable, you can 100% reach points that are basically unwinnable through your own actions. Maybe you sold a card you desperately needed for that elite encounter, or maybe you didn't sell enough of your cards and you got stuck with overloaded hands. It sucks, but every single time it felt like a learning experience when I lost or hit one of these brick walls.

Every character except maybe the Watcher has 2-3 distinct playstyles, but you generally mix and match with what you get via gaining knowledge of card synergies. For instance, the Ironclad could use Barricade and stack his blocks to the high heavens before landing a devastating Body Slam, or he could watch the world burn by playing Fiend Fire. Both options are equally fun, and the gameplay is flexible enough to allow you to pull off both if you know what you’re doing.

So far I fell like it is too RNG based for my tastes, my second run of the game was basically God tier and every run since is either doomed to failure from the get go or hits some random road block. 

I feel like an addict while playing because all I want is the high of that good run, but nothing compares.

Edit: just had another god run with a different character and honestly this game feels like gambling, pure luck, minimum strat.
Fun game, I am generally not a huge fan of card games and also didn't played a lot of rogue like games before, but this one I am actually enjoying a lot.

nice game lots of battery life on steam deck 
very satisfying to beat and every run is so diff

The game is fun. at least at a first glance, but I ended up losing interest to it because luck seems to prevail in regard to strategic/deck building skills.

I can understand some players may find the above statement questionable or even enjoy the amount of randomness in the game, however I find it unbelievable it lacks so many elemental tools such as an undo botton, hence my negative review.

Very fun game but ultimately not worth it aside from those who love being a glutton for punishment and then some. I've played games my entire life where when you die you lose 100+ hours worth of time in seconds. As fun as it is to win this, it isn't nearly as rewarding (i.e. fun) between replays unless you absolutely love deck builders and min-maxing - then this is for you.

Started out slow and confused, but picked it up on another day and can't stop playing now. Managed to complete the story with all characters and the game still feels fresh and has a lot to offer. Will also explore the Modding part. Definetely recommend, will keep you hooked for a while.

I have an unhealthy predisposition for roguelike deckbuilders. This obsession seems to be shared by indie developers as well, which has allowed games of this nature to dominate my Steam wishlist and in turn my library.

Yet, only with the recent release of Slay the Spire 2 (StS2) and a historical discount for Slay the Spire 1 (StS); did I finally take the time to experience the game that popularized roguelike deckbuilders in the digital gaming zeitgeist.

[spoiler]I emphasize 'popularized' so as not to disregard the contributions from earlier progenitors like Dream Quest or Dominion. That said, while there are plenty of direct ancestors, card games as a whole have existed across humanity in a variety of forms (from poker to Pokemon). I think there is something instinctual in the inherent popularity of the genre.[/spoiler]

Overall, I would sum up my experience as descriptively whelmed.

In the years since StS' initial release, there have been plenty of games inspired by it & innovated upon it. I have played many of those and, by rhyme & rote, I have been experiencing the fundamentals of StS again & again. In my patience for a good deal, I have spoiled my appetite.

StS continues to be well polished, well balanced, and well supported by its community. One may goes as far as to argue that it's the perfect roguelike deckbuilder. Its base formula is almost austere, which has allowed it to template core mechanics (i.e. branching paths, enemy intents, etc.) that would be used & reused by its descendants. But I think this deliberate statisical simplicity is ultimately a point-in-time. That is to say, the genre has continued to evolve beyond StS.

No better example of this evolution can be found in the sequel, StS2. If StS was indeed perfect, then the developers would have surely duplicated the original, adjusted the aesthetics, and called it a day. Of course, much of the core gameplay is the same but there have been deliberate mechanical changes (i.e. energy generation is more card reliant than relic focused) that I would argue have changed the experience significantly.

Just because StS did a great many things well, doesn't mean we've reached the pinnacle of what a roguelike deckbuilder could be. I look forward to my genre library growing ever bigger.
I'm only 39 hours into this game, but I finally understand the "hook" behind the roguelike deck builder. It's incredibly satisfying once you get a proper build going in this game

With such rave reviews, I honestly thought there would be more to this game.  No real story, grind your way to the top, maybe get some new cards, maybe upgrade some cards. Then rinse and repeat.  It was rather easy finding the right cards to breeze up, and then there was naught to do but level those cards.  Got boring real fast.

I suppose if repetitive, and simple games are your thing, then all the power to you.

So personally I've come to realize that games like this where you try your best to build a deck with synergy, you give yourself every possible chance to discover the cards you need, and still continually fail due to RNG on selection... they just aren't for me. If you read this and immediately thought of another frustrating experience you've had with another game, this game may not be for you either.

Games like this keep you coming back, "man if only I had that double damage card," "I didn't have enough damage mitigation this round I'll get more next time," "those relics were interesting but didn't help my deck," and "maybe those cards I just unlocked will appear and help me." Its that potential you can clearly see that keeps you coming back, but unfortunately almost every time you won't find what you need to succeed. Eventually you will, but its like that Unfair Flips game where you flip a coin until you get 10 heads in a row, only this one is with cards.
Craptastic! If you love having a rng heavy game with zero control play like some drunk came up with this idea for a game, that's probably a better alternative compared to this one.

Mir ist das ganze einfach viel zu random, um damit wirklich Spaß zu haben.

As a player who's never tried a deck-built game before, I really enjoyed STS. The base mechanics are pretty easy to understand, yet every card feels unique and adds a lot of depth to the multiple strategies you can try. Thanks to its roguelike format, the game becomes very addictive, and 2 runs are never the same. The game demands a bit of thinking and proper strategy, but it's not hard to reach the base endings. Although, reaching the true endings can become a harder challenge that will ask you for a proper strategy and a lot more experience. Finally, its art style is unique and charming, making the world and its lore very compelling to navigate.

Slay the Spire is one of the best roguelike deckbuilders out there. Its exceptionally designed and balanced gameplay makes it truly addictive to play, and slowly perfecting your strategy over the course of a run by strengthening your deck, and exploring card and relic synergies, is incredibly satisfying. The game also has an insane amount of content with four distinctly different playable characters with each their own separate card pool, which can even be expanded on through the steam workshop with quality fan made content expansions. It's a game I keep coming back to even after long periods of not playing, and I would personally recommend it to anyone (even if you usually wouldn't be into these kind of games).
ah yes my favorite exhaustion simulator. best game to play on SteamDeck

It is ugly as hell. But it's a good game. It's old and outdated though; a tried and true classic.
Get mods with hot bods.

The music is okay, but yeah, you're playing this one because you probably heard about its mainstreaminess.

This is my first deck builder game. Having a action/souls like gaming background i never would have expected to like this. But i found this game on sale and give it a shot. To my surprise i loved it from the first run. I was engaged the whole time and never i felt like the game was wasting my time. There is no fluff in this game, only gameplay, from minute 1. If you need a break from triple A games where nothing happens for hours, this is the game for you. Even if you think you won't like it, try it anyway. This is seriously one of the best gaming experience i've ever done.
I've never played a game before where I felt like over the course of 20 hours I got worst at it. Likely a skill issue or just a boring 1 hour rng fest. Hmmm...

got over 200 hours of spire slaying and still grind for completion with every character. people with terrible RNG luck like me will have to spend quite some time to get every achievement. but I'm not complaining, love me a game with close to endless replay value. easily one of the best deckbuilders out there. 
this is coming from someone who didn't like turn based combat in the past and also thought that this games design looked kinda ♥♥♥♥. I don't agree with these opinions anymore, pretty much did a 180 soon as I tried it out. buy slay the spire 💯

When I lose in Mario Kart it doesn't make me feel cheated. It's when I win and didn't even try that the series bugs me the most. I think I have a fundamental issue with the purposeful employment of chance in games, not because it will randomly screw me over, but that it will randomly fling me over the finish line.

There is certainly something inherently addictive in a gambling type way of winning the lottery and receiving an item you wanted, and that's why it's easy to lose hours and hours and hours to a game that expertly capitalises on those desires. Combine it with the tried and true elements of turn based RPG combat - synergies and stacking and counter tactics - and you have a triumphant game. For me it's irrefutable that Slay the Spire is a great game.

However, it's more difficult to answer whether I, rolling credits 35 hours later, truly enjoyed it. I finished it because I upgraded one card with infinite upgrades to do massive damage, then lucked out and got an artefact that played a card twice every turn, doubling that damage. Was that me? Did I finish the game? Did my accumulated knowledge and tuned strategy net me a victory? No. Absolutely not. And when you strive toward a goal it's downright a little awkward when you reach it by complete chance. Almost by accident, really.

Do other people have a more meaningful climb that does make it feel earned? Do people learn how to roll with their dealt hand in a way that the result is genuinely of their making? I'm totally sure of it.

I just have a brain thing where I appreciate a fixed challenge much much much more than what this ultimately in many cases amounts to. It made me yearn for no-nonsense JRPG dungeon crawlers like early Shin Megami Tensei and fondly remember nail biting boss fights that I meticulously strategised myself through. At first I was excited that I really was enjoying this, and the way that opened the door to a different genre, but the aspects that often make me bounce off the roguelike format slowly crept back into my consciousness, culminating in the way I eventually "beat" it.

I'm back to thinking this may not be my type of thing after all. But I'll keep you posted. :>

this game is beating my ass hard, but i keep comin back for that ass whoopin!! Diverse characters that are so wildly different from each other playstyle wise, but lots of familiarity in the cards you can pull through them all

The watcher is the best character ever conceived

idk why i took so long to try this game out... I blame my friend Ed for not telling me sooner.
10 spires out of 10

RNG is already bad. But once you get to the corrupt heart it will just cheat. Supposed to do 60 damage to me, I have more than enough block to stop it, not to mention also over 50 hp on top of that. 

"OOPS YOU DIED"

pros - incredibly fun and non-repetitive gameplay, each run is unique
cons - sometimes you have to do maths

I have almost 40 hours in sts2. Over the patches I felt like the game was ridiculous. Like. all the enemy's have wayyy too much health, they all do way too much damage, the characters are all very weak feeling. I must be crazy. then. they pushed a patch from beta. it made the doorman like. 5x harder but more imporantantly. the patch causes crashing. it was known about and reported. along with the other things people said wsas too much. It was pushed to live unchanged and made the game pretty much unplayable for me this weekend. Well. I said why not. Booted up this game. yeah. Sts2 is ridiculous. It's just unfun with how hard it is. 

but to talk about *this game* in my review. 

this game does everything right. The approach isn't "punish. punish. try to control exactly how you play. Punish." 

This game is "here's a challenge. Figure it out." 

That is waayyy more fun. Every choice in this game matters. There's no huge rng roll that kills you in this game. You build a deck. and build it well. You can overcome any challenge. It's not easy. But it's a challenge meant for you to overcome with good choices, experience, and smart plays. 

This is the good one. I'm going back to playing this for now.

It's a luck simulator.  I can see how people can find it addicting, in the same way gambling is addicting.

However, I don't like gambling.  The only decision you really make is:
-Which enemy to attack first.
-Which order to play your cards in.

If that excites you, cool.  It's boring to me, though.

I made a Steam account because my friend recommended this game to me. I then shocked him with how many hours I logged in game just in the first few weeks. I LOVEEEE this game and I could play it forever and ever.

Sure, the overall experience is good and you quickly get the hang of it, endless replayability, good music, and so on.

But it's literally a luck-based game, with a side of thinking. And, it's extremely punishing.

I am not putting any more time into it because I know I'm not going to have fun doing it.

5/10

It's fun for a while but you quickly notice how your runs are entirely dependent on RNG.
It's one of those games where the game plays you.

I don't get the hype for this one.

It's just a card game with artwork that looks like something your friend drew in middle school while bored in class.

Some runs the combos are comboing and it's all fun. Other runs feel like a slow downward spiral and you wonder if quitting now would make you a smart man or a pssy. 

Fun rounds are fever and rounds are waay too long to be unfun.

The only reason I played it this much is that this is one of the very few games that actually runs on my MacBook (props for that!) so this was my best option for entertainment while visiting home during the winter holidays. Otherwise I probably would have quit after 2-3 hours.
Bought it because of the glaze, thought i would hate it, but i played for 15 hrs the first two days i had it. Enjoyed it more than the triple A games i've played
Slay the Spire is a wonderful deck building turn based combat game. Fast to learn. Fun to explore various strategies. Easily worth checking out.
Played a lot of STS clones before actually playing the original, and none of them ever came close to this.
Game is a complete coin flip. Doesn't matter what you do, the boss will just decide its time for you to go and you are cooked. Fun game some times, but feels like it is pure luck.
You must have a specific taste in games to like this game but if you do then for sure you will play it for 20h minimum.10/10 for me

A game that suffers a little from over-balancing... like you can tell 100000 players' data was used to determine the parameters of the RNG. Thus, working towards a broken build is a rarity, but rather it becomes more about hanging on until you're surprised with RNG that turns into a winning strategy, IF you act upon it... 

And If you don't happen to fall into that specific RNG you can often end up in unwinnable battles. And that's fine to an extent... 

It adds an element of realness where things don't always goes your way. Y'know where many games are the opposite where as long as you excise basic motor skills and enter desired prompts, your hand is pretty much held and guaranteed success.

There is enough content, mechanics, variety and challenge to warrant Slay The Spire as a very rewarding and engaging game. But some of the RNG elements do peek at you from behind the curtain a little to make you aware that the game is not naturally-occurring but indeed a construct.... Which I think takes away a little from the players' triumphs because of the level of RNG dictation.

I still rate it very highly as the polish of completion of the product offers great value and a break from traditional gaming tropes of what constitutes gaming, but of course at the time of writing the card-battler genre has seen many pretenders rise to the fore, and in light of that Slay The Spire still shines beyond the pretenders who failed to innovate or offer the same quality of experience.

It kept my attention for the first 7 hours, until I realized it’s more of an addictive loop and I wasn’t really enjoying it anymore, just playing to try new combinations and strategies. But I’m a millennial, so unfortunately I’ve got my shields up against cheap dopamine, haha.

Something about this game scrapes at my skin. It's hard to explain, but I just hate everything about it.

I like the potential this game promises more than the actual game. I've had decks that were fun, and I really like the deckbuilding aspect, but I find the combat more tedious than enjoyable and this game is mostly combat with a tiny bit of deckbuilding. I revisited this with all the hype for StS2 to see if my attitudes have changed, but they have not: installed and uninstalled within the hour.
Game maybe good but so much time pass and borderless fullscreen still not fixed. Somehow in windows its looks on wrong screen DPI and no way to override it in the game.
A great game. Visuals are not as great as Slay the Spire 2, but it's still very good.

Pretty much everything that's been said about StS has already been said.

I really wanted to love this game.  But the hard ceilings in difficulty spikes meant that the lack of progression ultimately made me get stuck and not want to continue investing time into this game.

There's surprisingly only 4 playable characters in Vanilla.  There's some procedural generation of the opponents and the pathways between the acts.  But there's also tons of repetition, as there's only 3 rotating opponents for most Final bosses & Elites among the acts, along with the regular hallway opponents.  This means that continued replays after failing over & over get very repetitive & stale very quickly.

This means that a lot of the "learning" & "skill" in this game is in fact memorization.  Memorization of boss & hallway attack patterns.  Memorization of whom to attack first in group encounters.  Memorization of the symbols that reveal the identity of the final boss.  Memorization of the potential order of the final bosses.  Memorization of what dozens of cards for each character does and when they become available.  Memorization of the nature of relics.  Memorization of synergies between cards & cards, between cards & relics.

I played hundreds of rounds at A0 and only ever beat Act 3 5x, once with each character and twice with the Ironclad.  It's hard to switch between characters for variety because so much of success is learning each character's deck containing 75 or so cards.  So changing between characters causes one to lose a lot of the foundational knowledge of the playstyle they just spent dozens of hours learning.

I never even came close to beating the final Act 4 boss in the Heart.  I simply was skillcapped playing Ironclad despite putting the equivalent of a workweek of hours into trying to improve and progress.

The community in general is not very helpful.  There's lots of braying about "git gud" and that the top 1% of streamers have high win rates, negating the effects of RNG.  So I watched dozens of YT videos, tried tons of different builds, stopped trying for stylized decks, tried picking up damage cards in Act 1 instead, tried planning for the Act 1 boss specifically instead, tried playing with thinner decks, tried applying the things they recommended to do, fighting more elites for more relics, using less rests at campfires to chance it at lower health etc.

None of it really helped.  At AO with ironclad, I could fairly consistently get deep into Act 3, but struggled to beat the Act 3 boss.  When I did build a deck strong enough to clear the game, I'd just get merked by the Heart instead.

I'm pretty sure I'm done with this game.  Even if I study tactics more from watching the top streamers & guides, my eventual reward for clearing the Heart of A0 will be to do it over and over again on higher difficulties with more frustration.  Or learning to build very specific meta decks that are successful according to better players, but stifle creativity in the game.  Or start over with one of the 3 other characters and end up at the bottom of the knowledge tree all over again.

I played some other StS clones over the years.  I didn't have this problem of getting skillcapped with a steep learning curve requiring such mim/max deckbuilding and playstyles as I did with this game.  I 100% some other roguelite deckbuilders that had their own frustrations & challenges.  But nothing like I encountered with StS.

It's too bad, because I did somewhat enjoy the mechanics & the potential of StS.  But I have to let it go even though I'm embarassingly still stuck at A0 because the learning curve, time investment, playstyle & future rewards/challenges just aren't conducive to how I want to invest my gaming time as a resource.

Edit:  So I got sucked back in and played another 18 hours, watched even more streamers play the Ironclad for tips, etc.

1) I was under the mistaken impression that clearing an Ascension also required beating the 4th Act Heart  and not simply clearing the 3 regular boss Acts.  This means that I actually beat A0 many times without realizing it before going on to try A1, but still took 80-100 hours to beat the AO Heart the first time.

2) After grinding 100 hours and watching tons of hours of streamers, I've now managed to beat the Heart on A0 & A1.  Overall though, I didn't find the accomplishment very rewarding as the end game screens are very anticlimatic.  And the next Ascension levels simply have the same enemy repetition but with more levels of buffs & frustration.

3)  Watching even more streamers, my subtle improvements really still apply to my OG comments about a lot of the skill improvement in this game being based in memorization.  A lot of the reason that the top streamers are successful is that they have memorized and can hedge hidden game mechanics that aren't well explained in the base game (chance of a potion drop in a hallway fight, the sequence & DPS of boss attacks, the chance of rare relic drops based on the art asset of hallway chests, etc.)

TL;DR - I've had a litttle more success and am still grinding begrudgingly.  But most of my OG critiques still apply.

The original "big" roguelite deckbuilder – and the most addictive of them all – offers a ton of replayability, build variety, and getting stuck on that classic bargaining phase of "one more run". My one complaint about StS has always been its low enemy diversity, but I think I've seen the light now and realised that there's a certain kind of satisfaction in having a limited pool of hallway/elite fights which you have to prepare your deck for. In comparison to card game giants like MTG, StS is fairly simple and straightforward, but there's enough complexity hidden under the hood to pick your brain for hours: from strict and purely deterministic infinite loops to unhinged randomness enabled by relics like Dead Branch and Prismatic Shard. Will definitely be checking the sequel once it's out of EA.

very good, not the best. a lot comes down to luck of what relics you get, but you do have input on that. same with what cards you draw. you really do have an impact on the outcomes, maybe i'm just salty that i was doing great, now i'm not doing as well. i do recommend it though
i have only played this game for 5 hours and it is genuinely amazing, there are so many mods that you can add for a challenge or just adding characters from other games, absolute peak.

Incredible deckbuilding card game. The runs are long and definitely get super hard as you keep progressing and beating the game several times. Worth it.

Slay the Spire is the ultimate deck-building drug. The math is tight, the synergies are cracked, and the "just one more floor" itch is real. Whether you’re crunching numbers for an infinite discard loop on the Silent or praying to the RNG gods for a Dead Branch/Corruption combo on the Ironclad, the strategic depth is endless. It’s a masterclass in risk-reward where every misplay is a learning moment and every win feels like you’ve successfully outsmarted the developer. If you haven't played this yet, your library is objectively incomplete.
★★★★★ ( 5 / 5 )

The rogue-lite genre has maybe three must play pillars.  Dwarf Fortress is one of the three tangentially.  Balatro barely misses the list despite being excellent.  However, beyond all reasonable doubt I can safely suggest that someone with no rogue-lite experience to plays either FTL or Slay the Spire.  I still believe that FTL is the superior of the two, but these two are quality products that will give you many hours of enjoyment rather than being a passing fad.  I have not unlocked the full experience yet, but it has captured my time.

Ten Point Scale
Graphics 8/10, the graphics do not get in the way of the game but is not outstanding.
Sound 8/10, the sound does not get in the way of the game but is not outstanding.
Gameplay 10/10, You will want to play your next hand, see the next room and go on the next run.  The game is perfectly made.
Replayability 10/10, Each run feels unique and fair.  I do not need softened numbers like in Fire Emblem and prefer some "unfair" rng to keep things grounded.  On the other hand, the game itself has drop rng that keeps things fresh rather than a true bad pull system.  Yes, you are dealing with a deck of cards but you are curating the deck size (rng variance) and its output.
90%, frankly round it up to 100%
